title: Control Flow
description: Master if/else, switch, for, while, and do-while constructs with a competitive programmer's eye — focusing on loop patterns, iteration techniques, and the exact behaviors that separate a 1400-rated coder from a beginner. Includes loop optimization patterns directly used in contest solutions.

sections.content: In competitive programming, conditionals are often written as **single-line expressions** to save time. But understanding the full forms first prevents subtle bugs. Know when to use `if/else if/else`, when a ternary is cleaner, and when a lookup table replaces a chain entirely.

sections.title: if/else Forms in CP Context

sections.label: CP Trick: min/max instead of if
sections.content: For clamping or taking the better of two values, `min()` and `max()` are cleaner than `if/else`: `ans = max(ans, candidate)` is the idiom for 'update answer if better'. This appears in hundreds of problems.
The `for` loop is the **most used construct in competitive programming**. You must know all its forms — standard, range-based, reverse, step-based — and recognize the patterns instantly.

sections.title: All for Loop Patterns

sections.content: Use `while` when the number of iterations is **unknown in advance** — processing input until EOF, binary search loops, or simulation. Use `do-while` when you need to execute the body **at least once** (rare in CP, but appears in digit processing).

sections.title: while Loop Patterns in CP

sections.label: Infinite Loop Guard
sections.content: In simulation problems, always add a **maximum iteration count** guard (`if (steps > 1e6) break`). Without it, a bug in your logic causes TLE (Time Limit Exceeded) instead of WA (Wrong Answer), making debugging much harder.
`break` and `continue` are **essential tools** for clean loop logic in contests. `goto` has a specific, accepted CP use case: breaking out of **nested loops**.

sections.title: break, continue, and goto Patterns

sections.content: In competitive programming, `switch` is used less than in other domains. But it appears in problems involving **state machines**, **command parsing**, and **direction handling** (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT). Know the fall-through behavior.

sections.title: switch in CP — Direction Problem

sections.content: These are the **fundamental loop patterns** that appear repeatedly in Codeforces problems. Recognizing them instantly saves valuable contest time.

sections.title: Pattern 1 — Prefix Sums
Pattern 2 — Find Maximum Subarray (Kadane's)
Pattern 3 — Count Pairs with Condition

sections.label: Complexity Check Habit
sections.content: Before submitting, always estimate your solution's complexity. A loop with `n ≤ 10⁵` and an O(n²) inner loop = 10¹⁰ operations — guaranteed TLE. Codeforces typically allows ~10⁸ operations per second.

sections.title: Exercise 1: Loop Tracing with break/continue
sections.explanation: The loop runs from 0 to 9. When `i=3`: `continue` skips printing 3, goes to next iteration. When `i=7`: `break` exits the loop entirely. So we print 0, 1, 2, skip 3, print 4, 5, 6, then stop at 7 (not printed). The trailing space after 6 is printed because `cout << i << " "` runs before the check at i=7.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Nested Loop Pattern — Triangle
sections.explanation: Outer loop: i goes from 1 to 4. Inner loop: j goes from 1 to i. So on row i, we print i stars. Row 1: 1 star. Row 2: 2 stars. Row 3: 3 stars. Row 4: 4 stars. Each row ends with `\n`. Each star is followed by a space.

sections.title: Exercise 3: FizzBuzz (Classic Interview + CP Warmup)
sections.approach: Use a `for` loop from 1 to N. Check divisibility by 15 FIRST (both 3 and 5), then by 3, then by 5. Otherwise print the number. The order of checks matters — checking 3 first and 5 first would give wrong output for multiples of 15.
sections.explanation: Checking `i % 15 == 0` first is critical — it catches the FizzBuzz case before the `% 3` or `% 5` checks would incorrectly match. Alternatively: `if (i%3==0) cout<<"Fizz"; if (i%5==0) cout<<"Buzz"; if (i%3!=0 && i%5!=0) cout<<i; cout<<"\n";` — this avoids the order problem but checks twice.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Count Digits (do-while application)
sections.approach: Use `do-while` since N=0 should give 1 (the loop must run at least once). Repeatedly divide by 10 and count iterations. Alternatively, use `to_string(n).length()` — elegant but slower for huge N.
sections.explanation: The `do-while` guarantees at least one iteration, correctly handling `N=0` (count=1). A regular `while (n > 0)` would never enter the loop for N=0 and give count=0 (wrong). The loop extracts and discards digits one by one: for 12345, iterations give n=1234, 123, 12, 1, 0 — that's 5 iterations.

rows: Bitmask DP, meet in the middle
Two pointers, prefix sums, linear DP

sections.label: The 10⁸ Rule
sections.content: Codeforces judges run ~10⁸–10⁹ simple operations per second. As a safe estimate, use **10⁸ operations per second**. If your estimated operation count exceeds this × time limit (usually 1–2 seconds), you need a better algorithm.
